question: Compare the 100% and minimum flow conditions from Table 5.1-2 and explain the impact on natural circulation as described in the text.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects essential systems and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and abnormal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module. The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and ensure fuel design limits are not exceeded during all operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and it incorporates features for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The system interfaces with several other subsystems, including emergency cooling and chemical control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation between primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides figures and tables with these limits to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how actuation valves are exercised and tested, including during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the initial in-situ test to verify the DHRS’s ability to remove heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the first installed module.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a and related regulatory guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, inspection, and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a critical pressure boundary to prevent the release of fission products and provides structural support for various reactor components, with all materials and fabrication processes adhering to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure long-term integrity.

edges.description: Section A discusses the establishment of steady state values for system parameters at various power levels (including 100% and minimum flow), while Section B describes operational steps and system configurations that affect or are affected by these flow conditions, connecting both sections through their impact on natural circulation performance.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance at various flow conditions, referencing steady state values that are established based on the design described in Section B, which outlines the reliance on natural circulation in the reactor coolant system.

Section A discusses how steady state values for system parameters are established to optimize performance at different power levels, while Section B describes the reliance on natural circulation in the reactor coolant system, connecting the flow conditions in Table 5.1-2 to the system's natural circulation capability.

Section A describes operational procedures and component states that affect coolant flow, while Section B outlines the design reliance on natural circulation in the reactor coolant system, connecting them through the impact of flow conditions (100% and minimum) on natural circulation performance.

Section A describes operational steps involving system components that prepare the reactor for conditions affecting natural circulation, while Section B provides the overall system context by stating that the reactor coolant system relies on natural circulation without pumps, connecting the operational details in A to the design basis in B.

Section A provides a system description including operational features relevant to flow conditions, while Section B evaluates system performance across different power levels, both of which are connected by the flow parameters (100% and minimum flow) that impact natural circulation as analyzed in the text.

Section A describes the function of the degasification line and reactor vent valves in removing noncondensible gases from the pressurizer during both normal and emergency operations, while Section B provides further operational details on how the degasification line and venting process are used to match pressures and prepare for opening the vent valves, thus Section B specifies the operational sequence and context for the components introduced in Section A.

Section A describes specific features (e.g., degasification line, RVVs) that support the RCS's function, while Section B provides the general design basis that the RCS relies on natural circulation for coolant flow, connecting the operational details in A to the overall system function in B.

Section B establishes steady state parameter values for different flow conditions (such as 100% and minimum flow) to optimize system performance, while Section A describes the design features (including the steam generators and DHRS) that enable natural circulation under those conditions, thus Section A provides the physical basis for the operational scenarios evaluated in Section B.

Section A outlines the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the steam generators and decay heat removal system, which are directly referenced in Section B's operational sequence describing how these components are manipulated (e.g., isolation of SGs, use of DHRS) during different flow conditions relevant to natural circulation.

Section A details the specific components and subsystems of the reactor coolant system, while Section B provides the general design basis emphasizing reliance on natural circulation, thus Section A specifies the physical implementation that supports the functional requirement described in Section B.

Section A describes the function of degasification and vent lines in the RCS, while Section B lists these same components as part of the RCS design, connecting the operational purpose in A to the physical system described in B.

Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at different power levels (including 100% and minimum flow) which Section A's operating procedures must consider to ensure reactor vessel integrity and compliance with technical specifications during various operating conditions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with technical specifications during various flow conditions, while Section B describes system component operations (such as feedwater recirculation and pressure adjustments) that are implemented as part of those procedures, connecting the procedural requirements to specific system actions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure safe reactor vessel operation under various flow conditions, while Section B describes the design reliance on natural circulation for coolant flow, connecting them through the operational management of flow conditions (such as 100% and minimum flow) to maintain reactor safety.

Section A describes the DHRS's design basis to remove decay heat under various flow conditions, while Section B explains how steady state parameters (such as 100% and minimum flow) are established to optimize system performance, directly connecting the flow conditions in Table 5.1-2 to the DHRS's ability to support natural circulation.

Section A outlines the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in removing decay heat under various flow conditions, while Section B describes the operational steps and system configurations (such as valve positions and water levels) that establish the boundary conditions for those DHRS flow scenarios, thus providing the context necessary to interpret the impact of 100% and minimum flow conditions on natural circulation.

Section A describes the DHRS's role in removing decay heat and ensuring safe shutdown, which depends on the natural circulation flow provided by the RCS as described in Section B, thus linking the DHRS's performance to the RCS's natural circulation capability under different flow conditions.

Section A provides specific ASME code compliance details for reactor vessel attachments, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits (which are influenced by such design and compliance details) are not exceeded, connecting the design implementation in A to the operational controls in B.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under various operating conditions, while Section B describes the Decay Heat Removal System's design to maintain safe cooling during post-trip and shutdown scenarios; both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining safety limits during different flow and heat removal conditions as referenced in Table 5.1-2.

Section B discusses how steady state values for system parameters, such as flow conditions at different power levels (100% and minimum), are established to optimize performance, which provides the operational context for the tests and inspections of DHRS components described in Section A.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components and valves, while Section B describes operational steps involving the DHRS and related system components during specific plant conditions, with both sections connected by their focus on DHRS system integrity and operational readiness.

Section B describes the design reliance on natural circulation flow in the reactor coolant system, which is impacted by the operational conditions (such as 100% and minimum flow) referenced in Table 5.1-2, while Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for components (like DHRS valves) that support the safe operation of this system.

Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B discusses system evaluation criteria including flow conditions that affect natural circulation, connecting them through the need for design compliance (A) to ensure safe and optimal system performance under varying flow conditions (B).

Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B describes operational procedures affecting system components during different flow conditions, both of which are connected through their impact on the ability to maintain safe natural circulation as required by regulatory standards.

Section A discusses regulatory compliance for overpressure protection and code cases, while Section B provides a general design description of the reactor coolant system that relies on natural circulation, with both sections connected through the design and regulatory requirements for the reactor coolant boundary.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor vessel and its components, while Section B discusses how system parameters (such as flow conditions from Table 5.1-2) are evaluated to optimize plant performance, directly connecting the vessel's design to operational criteria like natural circulation.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its role in the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies operational procedures and system component interactions (such as the pressurizer and steam generators) that directly affect flow conditions relevant to natural circulation.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor vessel as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines the design basis that relies on natural circulation within this system, directly connecting the vessel's configuration to the natural circulation performance under different flow conditions.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, referencing the BPVC and OM Code, while Section B outlines compliance with BPVC and OM Code provisions for overpressure protection, indicating that both sections are connected through their reliance on ASME BPVC and OM Code standards for regulatory compliance.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, including valves and piping, which are part of the reactor coolant system described in Section B as being contained within the reactor pressure vessel and associated piping.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards for overpressure protection and integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the physical configuration and function of the reactor vessel as a pressure-retaining component subject to those same codes and standards.
